w:r.w:t: This document is the complete build-ready logic and scoring specification for the V2 platform. Every algorithm, calculation, and data processing decision the platform requires is defined here in pseudocode, plain SQL, and TypeScript logic. Claude Code receives this document before writing any scoring, analytics, framework content, or phase advancement logic — no logic decisions are made during the build.

Documents 7–12 are locked. This document supersedes Document 7 on one point: the cerv_floor_relief_test column type changes from BOOLEAN to VARCHAR to support graduated scoring. This correction is noted explicitly in Section 1.
Phase 1 Module Scoring Algorithms — TMJ and cervical raw scores, normalised scores, intake import rules, overlapping indicator logic
Profile Type Classification & Protocol Assignment — 7-type decision tree, protocol booleans, option recommendations
Edge Cases & Asymmetry Pattern Logic — low confidence, single strong movement, contralateral pattern, asymmetry classification
Profile Paragraph Generation Logic — assembly structure, findings summary, contextual flag modifier stacking
Framework Content System Architecture — directory structure, exercise interface, session construction algorithm, first-view logic
Daily Focus Line Assignment Logic — lookup structure, Phase 3 special handling, weekly resistance rotation
Phase Advancement & Access Control — sequential gate, all five phase advancement triggers, session increment
Analytics Calculations — all SQL queries and TypeScript logic for streaks, personal bests, Pearson correlation, all insight types
Contextual Nudge Triggers & Scoring Thresholds Constants File — complete /content/scoring-thresholds.ts

The Phase 1 deep assessment produces two raw scores — one for the TMJ module and one for the cervical module — which are then normalised as percentages of their respective module maximums. These normalised scores drive the profile type classification in Section 2. All scoring logic runs server-side. All threshold values are named constants imported from /content/scoring-thresholds.ts — never hardcoded inline.

The cerv_floor_relief_test column is defined as BOOLEAN in Document 7. This must be changed to VARCHAR(10) to support graduated scoring (3 points for clear reduction, 1 point for slight reduction). The column stores one of three string values:
'clear' — clear tinnitus reduction after 10 minutes supine → 3 points
'slight' — slight tinnitus reduction after 10 minutes supine → 1 point
NULL is treated as none (0 points). Update the Supabase column type to VARCHAR(10) on the first day of the build before any Phase 1 scoring logic is written.
**Progressively** — tmj_raw_score and cerv_raw_score are recalculated and written to phase1_assessment on every module save. This keeps stored scores current as the member works through modules.

**Finally** — when the member completes the asymmetry module and confirms their profile, both raw scores and both normalised scores are calculated one final time. profile_type, tmj_protocol_assigned, cerv_protocol_assigned, and profile_paragraph are all written at this point. completed_at is set. After this, scores do not change.
The profile type classification (Section 2) and all edge case logic (Section 3) run only at final scoring — not progressively.

Four indicators exist in both the intake test import and the Phase 1 in-platform history question. These are the same underlying symptom captured at two different stages. They must not double-count. The rule is:

For each of the four overlapping indicators: score from the Phase 1 assessment column if it has been answered (NOT NULL). If the Phase 1 column is NULL — meaning the member has not yet reached that question — fall back to the intake import value.
The Phase 1 answer is always the more carefully considered response and supersedes the intake import for scoring purposes once entered.
Module maximum: 30 points. 11 scoring indicators. 5 high-specificity at 4 points each, 4 moderate at 2 points, 2 general at 1 point.
Jaw drift on opening — consistent deviation in one direction
M1 — jaw opening modulation (any positive response)
M2 — jaw protrusion modulation (any positive response)
S5 — jaw clicking or popping (any positive response)
S2 — morning jaw soreness on waking (any positive)
Tinnitus noticeably worse after prolonged chewing
Daytime clenching (Phase 1 or S1 fallback — see 1.2)
Jaw pain eating or yawning (Phase 1 or S6 fallback — see 1.2)
All inputs are boolean — any positive response scores the full point value. No partial scores within an indicator. NULL evaluates to FALSE and contributes 0 points.
// ── General — 1 point each (overlapping indicator rule applies) ────
// Daytime clenching: Phase 1 if answered, else S1 intake fallback
// Jaw pain eating/yawning: Phase 1 if answered, else S6 intake fallback
RETURN ROUND(normalisedScore, 2) // Stored as NUMERIC(5,2) e.g. 66.67

Module maximum: 28 points. 11 scoring indicators. 3 high-specificity at 4 points each, 2 moderate-high at 3 points each (one graduated — see note), 4 moderate at 2 points each, 2 general at 1 point each.
Important note on floor lying relief test:

This indicator is graduated. The member selects one of three responses. These are mutually exclusive — only one scores. The cerv_floor_relief_test column stores 'clear', 'slight', or 'none' (VARCHAR — schema correction noted in 1.1).
M3 — neck curl on floor (any positive response)
Floor lying relief test — clear reduction ('clear')
Floor lying relief test — slight reduction ('slight')
Phase 1 assessment — cerv_floor_relief_test (same column, mutually exclusive)
Tinnitus noticeably worse after desk work or driving
Asymmetric SCM OR upper trapezius tenderness (either is sufficient — not additive)
Forward head posture confirmed via side profile
Neck pain or stiffness (Phase 1 or S7 fallback — see 1.2)
Cervicogenic headaches (Phase 1 or S8 fallback — see 1.2)
// Note: m4_asymmetric is a separate flag recorded for profile output.
// It does not add additional points to the raw score.
// ── Floor lying relief test — graduated (mutually exclusive) ───────
// SCM and upper trap share one 2-point slot — OR logic, not additive
IF assessment.cerv_scm_asymmetry = TRUE OR assessment.cerv_trap_asymmetry = TRUE
// ── General — 1 point each (overlapping indicator rule applies) ────
// Neck pain: Phase 1 if answered, else S7 intake fallback
// Cervicogenic headaches: Phase 1 if answered, else S8 intake fallback

When a member begins Phase 1, a single server-side query reads their intake test columns from the users table and pre-populates the relevant phase1_assessment fields. S3 and S4 are not imported into either module score — they remain general intake data only.
Superseded by tmj_daytime_clenching once Phase 1 entered
Superseded by tmj_pain_eating once Phase 1 entered
Superseded by cerv_neck_pain once Phase 1 entered
S8 — headaches at base of skull
Superseded by cerv_cervicogenic_headaches once Phase 1 entered
// ── Progressive save — on every module form submission ─────────────
// ── Final save — on profile generation confirmation ─────────────────
// Runs once. Locks all scores and writes profile output.
// and generateProfileParagraph are defined in Sections 2 and 4.
If a Phase 1 column is NULL (member has not answered that question), it evaluates to FALSE and contributes 0 points. No error is thrown.

If intake test columns are NULL (member has not taken the intake test), those imports contribute 0 points. The member is prompted to take the intake test before Phase 1, but this is a UX prompt — the scoring logic does not hard-block on missing intake data.
cerv_floor_relief_test NULL is treated as 'none' — 0 points.
Scores calculated progressively will be lower than final scores until all modules are complete. This is expected and correct behaviour.
Section 2 — Profile Type Classification & Protocol Assignment

Profile type is determined by comparing the two normalised scores after both modules are complete. The decision tree evaluates conditions in strict top-to-bottom order — the first condition that matches determines the profile type. This ordering is critical: without it, a member with TMJ 65% and cervical 35% could match multiple conditions simultaneously.
Clear jaw driver, negligible cervical involvement
Clear cervical driver, negligible jaw involvement
Both pathways meaningfully and equally active
Jaw leads clearly, substantial cervical involvement
Cervical leads clearly, substantial jaw involvement
Jaw leads, mild cervical involvement present
Cervical leads, mild jaw involvement present

Document 7 listed 5 profile type values. Document 13 uses 7. The dominant driver is encoded directly in the profile_type string (e.g. TMJ_PRIMARY_STRONG_SECONDARY vs CERV_PRIMARY_STRONG_SECONDARY). This means session construction, profile paragraph selection, and protocol option recommendations can all switch on profile_type alone without a secondary score comparison. Every place in the codebase that needs to know which side is dominant reads profile_type directly — no additional logic required.
Evaluated top-to-bottom. First match wins. All threshold values are named constants from /content/scoring-thresholds.ts.
// Clean single-driver cases take priority. A member with TMJ 65%
// and cervical 35% would technically satisfy "primary with strong
// secondary" but is correctly classified as TMJ_DOMINANT first.
// Both scores meaningful AND close together.
// One score clearly leads (> 50%), secondary is substantial (30–50%).
// One score leads (> 30%), secondary present but minor (20–30%).
// Reached only when both scores are below all meaningful thresholds.
// In practice: both scores below 20%. Assign whichever is higher.
// This case also triggers the low-confidence edge case in Section 3.
// If both are identical, TMJ_DOMINANT by convention.

These booleans are set separately from profile type. They drive the session construction algorithm in Section 5 and the "In your protocol" tag in the exercise library. A protocol can be assigned even if that module is not the primary driver — the threshold is intentionally low.
// Both booleans are set to TRUE regardless of scores.
// See Section 3.1 for low-confidence detection logic.
// Low-confidence assignment overrides the standard threshold check.
These booleans are written to phase1_assessment at profile generation and copied to framework_progress on Phase 1 completion, where they are read by session construction throughout Phases 3 and 4.
2.4 — Protocol Option Default Recommendation by Profile Type

The protocol option (1 = Sequential, 2 = Parallel, 3 = Prioritised Parallel) is chosen by the member in the profile output screen. The platform recommends an option based on profile type. The member's selection is stored as framework_progress.protocol_option.
Single driver. Low daily load. Clean variable control.
Single driver. Low daily load. Clean variable control.
Both pathways equally active. Simultaneous is most efficient.
Full primary + 2–3 key secondary. Best balance for this profile.
Full primary + 2–3 key secondary. Best balance for this profile.

Note: PRIMARY_SECONDARY_LEAD is functionally equivalent to DUAL_DRIVER_MIN_SCORE (both > 30) in the current tree. They are defined as separate constants so they can be adjusted independently as outcome data accumulates.
Section 3 — Edge Cases & Asymmetry Pattern Logic

Four edge case checks run at profile generation, after both module scores are calculated and before the profile type is finalised. They run in a defined order. Each check either modifies the profile output text, adds a flag to the profile, or (in the case of low confidence) modifies the protocol assignment itself.
3.1 — Edge Case 1: Low Confidence — Both Scores Below Threshold

This is the most important edge case. A member with both normalised scores below 20% has not produced confirmatory physical test findings in either module. The assessment did not generate a clear somatic picture.
// Sub-case A: symptoms suggest somatic involvement despite low physical scores
// Sub-case B: low signal across the board

"Your symptom picture suggests somatic involvement — your tinnitus responds to jaw and neck-related triggers and varies in ways consistent with a somatic driver. However, the physical movement and palpation tests in this assessment did not produce strong confirmatory findings. This can happen for a few reasons: body awareness develops with practice, some people do not respond strongly to initial palpation, or the physical findings may be subtler in your case.

The framework is still worth pursuing. We recommend completing Phase 2 and beginning Phase 3 with a light exploratory protocol covering both modules, while staying attentive to whether the physical work produces noticeable changes. Reassessing after four weeks of protocol work often produces clearer findings as body awareness improves."

"Your assessment did not produce strong findings in either the jaw or cervical modules. This does not rule out a somatic component — some people find that body awareness and palpation sensitivity develop over time, and a reassessment after a few weeks of protocol work often produces clearer results.

If you are uncertain whether somatic tinnitus applies to you, Phase 1 of the framework is the right place to start regardless — the mechanism education and self-assessment process itself is valuable. You can retake this assessment at any point from the exercise library."
When either low-confidence variant fires, the standard protocol assignment logic is overridden:
Session construction routes to buildLowConfidenceList() — see Section 5.5
Session structure choice (protocol option) is skipped at profile output — protocol_option set to NULL
Profile output Section 6 (session structure choice screen) is omitted for low-confidence members
A reassessment prompt appears at the four-week mark in Phase 3 — implemented as a contextual nudge
3.2 — Edge Case 2: Single Strong Movement Response

Fires when a high-specificity indicator scored its maximum 4 points while the overall module score remains below the dominant threshold. Does not override profile type — adds an explanatory note to Section 2 of the profile output (Specific Findings Summary).
// Check for strong TMJ indicators within a lower overall score
// Check for strong cervical indicators within a lower overall score
RETURN result // Empty arrays = flag does not fire
Output text when fired (inserted into Specific Findings Summary)

"One finding worth highlighting: [jaw drift / M1 response / pterygoid tenderness / suboccipital tenderness] is a high-specificity indicator — it points directly at [trigeminal pathway / upper cervical pathway] involvement. Even within your overall score, this finding is meaningful and is reflected in your protocol assignment."
The bracketed values are resolved from the result array returned by the function.

Fires when jaw drift is confirmed in one direction and tinnitus is louder in the opposite ear. The plausible mechanism is lateralised pterygoid overactivity generating asymmetric trigeminal input. This has not been systematically studied — the framing is explicitly exploratory.
// Contralateral: drift direction opposite to worse ear
Output text when fired (appended to asymmetry section)

"Your assessment identified a potentially significant asymmetric pattern: your jaw drifts to the [left/right] on opening, and your tinnitus is louder in the [right/left] ear — the opposite side. There is a plausible mechanism for this pattern through lateralised pterygoid overactivity generating asymmetric trigeminal input to the dorsal cochlear nucleus. However, this specific pattern has not been systematically studied and its consistency across individuals is not established. Treat this as a finding worth exploring rather than a confirmed mechanism. Your Phase 3 jaw protocol applies asymmetric emphasis toward the [drift side] regardless — this finding reinforces that emphasis."
Runs after all four modules are complete, immediately before profile generation. Consolidates all asymmetric findings into one of five pattern outputs.
// Note: CONTRALATERAL_PATTERN is not returned here — it is applied as an
// additive flag on top of whichever pattern above applies.
Multiple asymmetric findings all point the same direction + unilateral tinnitus
Asymmetric protocol emphasis toward dominant side throughout Phase 3
Multiple asymmetric findings all same direction but bilateral/central tinnitus
Side-specific emphasis applied, lower confidence in lateralisation
Protocol addresses each finding individually — no single-side emphasis
Noted in profile, mild side emphasis where relevant
Protocol bilateral throughout — both sides worked equally
3.5 — Edge Case Check Order at Profile Generation
// 1. Low confidence — may override protocol assignment
// 2. Single strong movement — adds note to findings summary
// 3. Contralateral pattern — set on assessment row, read here

The profile output screen is the most important single screen on the platform. Every member sees a different output based on their specific combination of scores, findings, and flags. The profile_paragraph TEXT field stored in phase1_assessment is assembled from six sections. The text templates for every section and variant are in Document 8 Phase 1 Part B.7. This section specifies the logic that selects and assembles them — not the text itself.
FUNCTION generateProfileParagraph(assessment, user, tmjNorm, cervNorm, edgeCaseFlags):
// Filter null sections (Section 6 omitted for low-confidence and pure single drivers)
Selects the opening statement directly from profile_type. The dominant driver placeholder is resolved from the profile type string. Full text for all seven variants is in Document 8 B.7 Section 1.

Generated dynamically from confirmed positive flags only. Categories with no confirmed findings are omitted entirely. This section lists only actual findings — it is never templated with placeholder text.
jawFindings.push('Jaw drift on opening — ' + assessment.tmj_jaw_drift_direction + ' deviation confirmed')
jawFindings.push('Jaw opening response positive on movement test')
jawFindings.push('Jaw protrusion response positive on movement test')
jawFindings.push('Morning jaw soreness or facial tension on waking')
jawFindings.push('Tinnitus noticeably worse after prolonged chewing')
cervFindings.push('Neck curl on floor response positive on movement test')
cervFindings.push('Head rotation response positive — asymmetric between sides')
cervFindings.push('Floor lying relief test — clear tinnitus reduction')
cervFindings.push('Floor lying relief test — slight tinnitus reduction')
cervFindings.push('Tinnitus noticeably worse after desk work or driving')
cervFindings.push('Restricted or asymmetric rotation range — restricted side: ' + assessment.cerv_restricted_side)
IF assessment.cerv_scm_asymmetry = TRUE OR assessment.cerv_trap_asymmetry = TRUE
cervFindings.push('Asymmetric SCM or upper trapezius tenderness')
maintainingFindings.push('Shoulder height asymmetry — elevated side: ' + assessment.post_elevated_side)
maintainingFindings.push('Asymmetric exercise or movement patterns confirmed')
4.4 — Section 3: Personalised Paragraph — Contextual Flag Modifier Stacking

The base paragraph template is selected by profile type, then contextual flags are applied on top in a defined stacking order. Each flag appends an additional sentence or modifies a bracketed placeholder. The full modifier text for each flag is in Document 8 Phase 1 B.7 Section 3.
// 1. Select base template by profile type
// 3. Apply TMJ contextual history flags (jaw-involved profiles only)
// 4. Apply cervical contextual history flags (cervical-involved profiles only)
nsFlags = countNsFlags(assessment) // count of TRUE values among 4 NS flags
// 6. Wrap in low-confidence framing if edge case fired
// 7. Append single strong movement note if fired
// [primary driver] → jaw or cervical — from profile_type
// [dominant side] → most consistently flagged side across findings
RETURN template with all placeholders resolved
4.5 — Sections 4, 5, and 6: Asymmetry, Protocol Assignment, Session Structure
// Full text for each profile type in Document 8 B.7 Section 5
// Full text and option descriptions in Document 8 B.7 Section 6
// Omitted (returns NULL) for low-confidence members and pure single drivers
// Omit for low-confidence members — they do not choose a protocol option
// Omit for pure single drivers where secondary is negligible

The framework content system uses a hybrid architecture. All member-facing written content lives in static TypeScript files in the /content directory. The database provides only the member's position, flags, and progress state. No framework content is fetched from the database — it is always read from static files at build time or import time.

Framework content lives in /content as TypeScript constant files. The database stores where the member is and what their profile is — not what they read. When building any framework content component, read content from /content files. Read member state from the database. Never store member-facing text in the database.
/phase4-exercises.ts ← Phase 4 postural and nervous system exercises
Every exercise object across all content files must conform to this TypeScript interface. Claude Code must use this exact interface for every exercise — no fields added or removed.
id: string // Document 8 section ref e.g. "D4_heat_application"
phase: number // Phase this exercise belongs to
durationSeconds: number // Used for /session time-remaining calculation
focusLine: string // One-line daily focus — used in condensed view
shortDescription: string // One line — exercise library card subtext
mechanismExplanation: string // Full mechanism rationale — first visit only
techniqueInstructions: string // Full step-by-step instructions — first visit only
briefInstructions: string // 2–3 line summary — condensed view
videoId: string // External video platform ID for embed
contraindications: string | null // null if none
profileModifiers: ProfileModifier[] // Conditional text blocks — may be []
condition: string // Flag name on phase1_assessment e.g. "tmj_jaw_drift"
conditionValue: any // Value that triggers this modifier e.g. true
text: string // Additional text rendered when condition is met

Phase 1, 2, 4, and 5 use static ordered session arrays — the same content in the same order for every member. Profile modifiers are applied within individual sections at render time, not by changing the session order.
id: string // Document 8 section reference e.g. "B1"
sectionKey: string // Key into the phase content object
name: string // Display name — shown in dashboard session list
{ id:'B1', sectionKey:'openingMechanism', name:'The Common Mechanism', estimatedMinutes:15, phase:1 },
{ id:'B2', sectionKey:'module1Tmj', name:'Module 1 — Jaw and TMJ', estimatedMinutes:20, phase:1 },
{ id:'B3', sectionKey:'module2Cervical', name:'Module 2 — Cervical and Suboccipital', estimatedMinutes:20, phase:1 },
{ id:'B4', sectionKey:'module3Postural', name:'Module 3 — Postural Factors', estimatedMinutes:10, phase:1 },
{ id:'B6', sectionKey:'asymmetryModule', name:'Asymmetry and Pattern', estimatedMinutes:10, phase:1 },
{ id:'B7', sectionKey:'profileGeneration', name:'Your Driver Profile', estimatedMinutes:10, phase:1 },
// Phase session counts — used in Section 7 session increment logic

Runs server-side on every load of /session. Reads five values from framework_progress and phase1_assessment, returns an ordered array of exercise IDs. Exercise content for each ID is then pulled from static content files.
// ── Low-confidence check — runs before protocol option branching ───
// Low-confidence members always get the minimal exploratory list.
// protocol_option is NULL for these members — skip option branching.
// Still add resistance if applicable (rare — low-confidence members
// are unlikely to reach resistance phase but logic must handle it)
// TMJ release runs until resistance phase starts,
// then cervical is added when TMJ moves to resistance.
// Determined by tmj_sequential_phase flag in framework_progress JSONB.
ELSE IF protocolOption = 2 // Parallel — both full protocols
...buildCervReleaseList(), // cervical first per combined session order
// Dual driver on Option 3 = same as Option 2
// ── Add resistance exercises if resistance phase active ────────────
// ── Add Phase 4 exercises if member is engaged with Phase 4 ───────
The exact exercise ID arrays for each build function. Exercise IDs match the id field on the Exercise interface (5.2) and Document 8 section references.
// Full TMJ release list — all members with tmj_protocol_assigned = TRUE
// D11 resting position is a daily habit — covered in content,
// not included in the exercise checklist
// Full cervical release list — all members with cerv_protocol_assigned = TRUE
// Reduced secondary cervical — for TMJ-primary profiles on Option 3
// Reduced secondary TMJ — for cervical-primary profiles on Option 3
// Low-confidence minimal exploratory list — both assigned, one each
When both protocols are included in a session, exercises are always ordered in this sequence regardless of which protocol is primary. This order works from the base of the structural chain upward.
Joint mobilisation (D9 TMJ distraction, E9 upper cervical mob)
Included within protocol order — no reordering needed

Content sections and exercises that have profile-specific variations follow this pattern at render time. Content files define all modifier variants. Components read member flags and select the correct block.
// In any content section React component:
// Example usage in a Phase 3 exercise component:
// All modifier text blocks live in /content/profile-modifiers.ts
// keyed by exercise ID and condition name.
// Claude Code reads modifier text from the content file.
// It never constructs modifier text dynamically in a component.

The exercises_viewed JSONB column on framework_progress tracks which exercises a member has completed at least once. First visit renders full content. All subsequent visits render the condensed view with an optional expand link.
// On exercise completion — first time only
// jsonb_build_object adds key without overwriting existing keys
// Operation is idempotent — setting true on an already-true key is harmless
// 1. Exercise name and daily focus line
// 3. Profile modifier block (conditional — rendered if relevant flag confirmed)
// 2. "Read full explanation" expand link (expands full content inline)
// Shape of session_in_progress JSONB on framework_progress:
FUNCTION saveSessionProgress(exerciseId: string, userId: string, frameworkProgress):
FUNCTION resumeSession(frameworkProgress): { completed: string[], isResume: boolean }
// Stale session from a previous day — clear it, start fresh
// On session completion — all exercises marked complete:
// No progress row — first framework access ever
// Phase 3 and 4 always go to daily session view
// sectionUrls is a static object in /content/session-lists/
// Edge case: session exceeds max for phase (phase completed, not yet advanced)
IF url IS NULL OR UNDEFINED
Section 6 — Daily Focus Line Assignment Logic

The Today's focus line on the dashboard is a single sentence pulled from a static content object keyed by phase and section. No database fetch is required. The dashboard reads current_phase and current_session from framework_progress and looks up the line directly.

All focus lines live in /content/focus-lines.ts. The full text for every line is in Document 8 across all phases. The structure below shows the shape — Claude Code populates the text values from Document 8 when building the content file.
3: "Honest assessment is the foundation of specific treatment",
4: "Understanding your pattern is the first act of changing it",
// ... focus lines from Document 8 Phase 2 content
release: "Release before you retrain", // Fixed — entire release phase
// Weekly rotation array — lines from Document 8 D.13–D.19 and E.12–E.16
// Indexed 0–N, cycled weekly from resistance_phase_start date
// Populated from Document 8 resistance phase focus lines
// ... focus lines from Document 8 Phase 4 content
// ... focus lines from Document 8 Phase 5 content
complete: "Maintain your protocol and trust the progress you have made",
// Phases 1, 2, 4, 5 — direct lookup by session number
// Edge case: session is at last section, phase not yet advanced
// Return the last defined focus line for the phase
IF focusLine IS NULL OR UNDEFINED
// Release phase only — resistance not yet acknowledged
// Same every day for the entire release phase — does not cycle
// Cycle on 7-day rotation, wrapping when array is exhausted
Returns fixed completion line: "Maintain your protocol and trust the progress you have made"
current_phase=1, current_session=1 → returns DAILY_FOCUS_LINES[1][1]. No special handling needed.
Phase completed, not yet advanced (e.g. Phase 2 all sessions read, checklist not submitted)
current_session is at max for phase. Fallback returns last defined focus line for that phase.
Member in Phase 3 but also engaging with Phase 4
Dashboard focus line reflects current_phase (Phase 3), not Phase 4. Phase 4 focus lines appear within Phase 4 content pages only.

Phase access is enforced in Next.js middleware on every /framework/phase-[N] request. Phase advancement is triggered by explicit member actions — never automatically except on Phase 1 profile confirmation and Phase 2 checklist submission.
Runs in middleware on every /framework route request. First line of defence — runs before any page content is served.
// Phase 4 exception — accessible from Phase 2 onwards regardless of position
// Locked — redirect silently, no error message shown to member
// Section access within an accessible phase:
// Members can read ahead freely within their current phase.
// All sections within an accessible phase are accessible.
// No per-section gate — phase-level gate is sufficient.
// Direct URL to locked phase (member types URL manually):
// Middleware catches this and redirects to current phase.
// No error message — redirect is silent.
Two sequential acknowledgements are required. The second acknowledgement is the actual trigger. Protocol option must be selected and written before phase advances.
// Trigger: member selects session structure option and taps "Confirm my session structure"
// This is the second acknowledgement — fires after profile output has been read.
// Write all phase 1 completion state in a single transaction
// Trigger Phase 1 completion email via EmailOctopus API route
// Low-confidence members — protocol_option is not selected.
tmj_protocol_assigned = TRUE, // Both assigned — overridden by low-confidence logic
Trigger: member submits the maintaining factor confirmation checklist (Document 8 Section C.8). No time gate. No minimum session count. Member decides when habits are genuinely in place.
// Phase 3 unlocks immediately on this write.
// Phase 4 also accessible from this point (current_phase >= 2 check in 7.1).
Manual. No automatic trigger. The complete button has two preconditions — both must be true before the button is active. The button appears on the Phase 3 overview page (/framework/phase-3).
// Condition 1: resistance phase must have been acknowledged
// Condition 2: minimum 4 weeks since Phase 2 completion
// When button is inactive — display with greyed state and this message:
// "Available after [date 4 weeks from phase2_completed_at] and once
// Permanent prompt beneath button regardless of elapsed time:
// "Most members find Phase 3 takes eight to sixteen weeks to produce
// meaningful and stable change. Mark complete when your physical
// indicators and progress tracker data reflect genuine improvement,
// not when a minimum has elapsed."
// Confirmation modal fires first — member confirms before this runs.
// Modal text: "Are you sure? Phase 5 covers stabilisation and maintenance.
// Phase 3 content remains fully accessible after advancing."
// Phase 4 remains accessible — no change to phase4 state.
// Phase 4 first access — written on first navigation to any Phase 4 content.
// Checked on every Phase 4 page load.
// Phase 4 optional completion — member taps "Mark Phase 4 complete"
// from Phase 4 overview page. Optional. No downstream effect.
// Outcome selection — required before G.1 acknowledge button activates.
// Member selects one of three radio options on G.1.
// G.1 acknowledge button is disabled until this value is written.
// Enforce client-side: button disabled until radio is selected.
// Phase 5 completion — fires when member acknowledges G.8
// (the section immediately before the final G.9).
// Dashboard Start today's session button changes to
// "View maintenance protocol" once phase5_completed_at IS NOT NULL.
// Fires when member taps the acknowledge button on:
// "I am ready to begin the resistance phase — add resistance exercises to my daily session."
// - Focus line shifts to weekly resistance rotation
Fires when a member completes a content section in Phases 1, 2, or 5. Idempotent — completing the same section twice does not double-increment.
FUNCTION incrementCurrentSession(userId: string, completedSessionId: string, frameworkProgress):
// Idempotency check — only increment if this is the current active session
RETURN // Already completed or out of order — do nothing
// If at last session of phase: do not auto-advance.
// current_session stays at max until the explicit phase advancement
// trigger fires (7.2–7.6). On phase advancement, current_session

All calculations run server-side. Data is fetched in parallel on /analytics page load and passed to the page as computed values — no client-side calculation. All queries assume progress_logs for the authenticated user unless noted. The time window selector on the analytics page filters graph display only — personal bests and correlation insights always use the full dataset.
— All progress_logs for this user ordered by log_date ASC (full dataset needed for all calculations)
— framework_progress row (phase completion timestamps, resistance_phase_start, started_at)
— phase1_assessment row (profile_type — for correlation insight profile context modifier)
Counts consecutive calendar days ending today (if logged today) or yesterday (if not). A gap of one day or more breaks the streak.
-- Start from today if logged today, otherwise yesterday
ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY log_date DESC) AS rn,
- (ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY log_date DESC) - 1) AS expected_date
// If most recent log is older than yesterday, streak is 0
// Anchor: start from today if logged, else yesterday
const anchor = sorted[0].getTime() === today.getTime() ? today : yesterday
-- Group consecutive dates by subtracting row number
-- Consecutive dates produce the same group value
log_date - (ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY log_date ASC))::integer AS grp
Used for both sub-4 and sub-3 personal bests. Pass $threshold = 4 for sub-4 streak, $threshold = 3 for sub-3 streak. Constants: SUB_FOUR_STREAK_THRESHOLD = 4, SUB_THREE_STREAK_THRESHOLD = 3.
-- Call twice: once with $threshold = 4, once with $threshold = 3
-- Running count of days where loudness exceeds threshold
-- Each exceedance increments the group counter, breaking the streak
SUM(CASE WHEN loudness > $threshold THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)
OVER (ORDER BY log_date ASC) AS grp
SELECT loudness AS value, log_date AS achieved_date
ORDER BY loudness ASC, log_date ASC
ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
PostgreSQL has a native corr() aggregate function. This is the primary implementation. TypeScript fallback provided for any environment where corr() is unavailable.
-- Pearson correlation between any metric and loudness
-- $metric is one of: jaw_tension, neck_tension, stress, sleep_quality
-- All four run on every /analytics page load.
-- Full dataset — never filtered by time window for correlation
-- Returns NULL if insufficient variance (all values identical)
-- Returns no rows if fewer than 14 logs
function pearsonCorrelation(x: number[], y: number[]): number | null {
const meanX = x.reduce((a, b) => a + b, 0) / n
const meanY = y.reduce((a, b) => a + b, 0) / n
if (denomX === 0 || denomY === 0) return null // No variance
return null // Below minimum threshold — insight not displayed
Better sleep → lower loudness (expected beneficial pattern)
sleep_quality is 1–10 where 10 = best. Negative r = beneficial direction.
Jaw tension does not increase loudness
Neck tension does not increase loudness
Run four times substituting $metric each time. Additional aggregate values (avg at high and low loudness) populate the specific threshold sentence in the insight card.
-- Run 4 times — $metric: stress, sleep_quality, jaw_tension, neck_tension
-- avg_metric_high_loudness and avg_metric_low_loudness are used to
-- "Your loudness scores tend to be higher on days when you rate
Display logic: insight card only rendered if |r| >= CORRELATION_MINIMUM_STRENGTH (0.3). Strength label and direction label derived from 8.6. Up to four insight cards shown — one per metric.
Compares average loudness before and after Phase 3 started. Only calculated if resistance_phase_start OR phase2_completed_at is set.
-- Average loudness before and after Phase 3 start
COUNT(CASE WHEN pl.log_date < p3.start_date THEN 1 END) AS days_before,
COUNT(CASE WHEN pl.log_date >= p3.start_date THEN 1 END) AS days_after
-- Near-zero = flat, use honest framing from Document 12 Section 5.8
-- Conditions on the 5 lowest and 5 highest loudness days
RANK() OVER (ORDER BY loudness ASC) AS low_rank,
RANK() OVER (ORDER BY loudness DESC) AS high_rank
-- "Your 5 lowest loudness days all had stress below [best_avg_stress]
-- and sleep above [best_avg_sleep]. Your 5 highest loudness days tended
WHEN log_date >= DATE_TRUNC('week', CURRENT_DATE) - INTERVAL '7 days'
COUNT(CASE WHEN log_date >= DATE_TRUNC('week', CURRENT_DATE) - INTERVAL '7 days'
-- Display "Not enough data" for that week (Document 12 Section 5.9)
Splits all logs into first half and second half of logging history to show whether distribution is shifting toward lower scores over time.
-- Only rendered if total log count >= 14
ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY log_date ASC) AS rn,
COUNT(CASE WHEN rn <= total / 2 THEN 1 END) AS earlier_count,
COUNT(CASE WHEN rn > total / 2 THEN 1 END) AS recent_count
-- Each row: two bar values for the Recharts BarChart component
8.12 — Profile Context Modifier on Correlation Insights
Additional note appended to a correlation insight card when a member's profile type and a strong correlation create a clinically meaningful combination worth highlighting.
// Cervical-dominant profile + strong jaw tension correlation
// Suggests interconnected jaw/cervical drivers worth noting
return "Given your cervical-primary profile, this jaw tension pattern is worth noting — it may reflect the interconnected nature of your jaw and cervical drivers."
// TMJ-dominant profile + strong neck tension correlation
return "Given your jaw-primary profile, this neck tension pattern is worth noting — it may reflect the interconnected nature of your jaw and cervical drivers."
Section 9 — Contextual Nudge Triggers & Scoring Thresholds Constants File
All five nudges share the same dismissal mechanism. Once dismissed, a nudge never reappears. Dismissal is stored in the nudges_dismissed JSONB column on framework_progress.
// Write on tap of X button on nudge card
// nudges_dismissed starts as {} (empty object) — see Document 12 Section 6.13

Each nudge has an ID, a trigger condition, a destination, display text, and a CTA label. Nudges render as highlighted inline cards within framework content at the trigger point. They appear once and dismiss independently.
// Trigger point: end of Phase 2 habits audit (C.2)
text: "Your assessment flagged sustained desk load. Phase 4's workstation section is directly relevant.",
// Trigger point: Phase 2 sleep foundations section (C.5)
text: 'Sleep position change is one of the highest-impact things you can do. Phase 4 covers this in full.',
// Trigger point: end of Phase 2 (C.8 maintaining factor checklist)
text: 'Phase 4 breath work takes 10 minutes daily and can produce noticeable benefit within 2–3 weeks.',
// Trigger point: day 14 of Phase 3 (from phase2_completed_at)
text: "Two weeks into your protocol — Phase 4's nervous system section addresses this directly alongside your protocol work.",
// Trigger point: day 14 of Phase 3 (same timing as nudge 4)
text: "Phase 4's attentional techniques can reduce perceived loudness independently of the physical driver work.",

Nudges 4 and 5 have identical timing (Day 14 of Phase 3) but different trigger conditions. If both conditions are true for a member, both nudges can appear simultaneously. Each dismisses independently — dismissing one does not affect the other.
All nudge trigger conditions are evaluated on every relevant page load. The isNudgeDismissed() check runs first — dismissed nudges are never re-evaluated.

This is the complete constants file as it must exist in the codebase. Every value in this file is used by name throughout the logic. Claude Code must create this file exactly and import from it whenever any of these values are needed.
// SOMATIC TINNITUS PROJECT — V2 Scoring Thresholds and Logic Constants
// Every value in this file is a named constant used throughout the codebase.
// Never write any of these values as inline numbers anywhere in the code.
// Always import from this file and reference by name.
// These values are designed to be adjusted post-launch as member outcome
// data accumulates. Changing a value here propagates everywhere automatically.
// A magic number requires finding every instance manually.
// Total possible raw score for each module. Used as normalisation denominator.
// Change only if indicators are added to or removed from a module.
// Normalised score above this = high driver influence = single driver candidate
// Normalised score below this = negligible = no protocol assigned
// Both scores must exceed this AND be within DUAL_DRIVER_MAX_DIFFERENCE
// Maximum gap between scores for dual driver classification
// Leading score must exceed this for "primary with strong secondary"
// Secondary score lower bound for "strong secondary" designation
// Secondary score upper bound for "strong secondary" designation
// (above this, scores are close enough for dual driver consideration)
// Intake test symptom_score at or above this value triggers the
// Number of confirmed NS flags (from 4 possible) that triggers:
// - High nervous system modifier in profile paragraph
// Minimum absolute Pearson r value to display a correlation insight card.
// r value at or above this = "Moderate" strength label on insight card
// r value at or above this = "Strong" strength label on insight card
// Minimum number of progress_logs required before any correlation
// Loudness thresholds for sub-N streak personal bests.
// A log day counts toward the streak if loudness <= this value.
// Minimum weeks from phase2_completed_at before the Phase 3 complete
// button becomes active. Does not auto-advance — controls button state only.
// Days into Phase 3 (from phase2_completed_at) before nervous system
// current_session values at which specific Phase 2 nudges trigger.
// Update these if Phase 2 session order changes.
PHASE2_HABITS_AUDIT_SESSION: 2, // C.2 — end of habits audit
// Type export for use throughout codebase
CRITICAL INSTRUCTION — READ BEFORE WRITING ANY LOGIC CODE
Every threshold value, minimum score, day count, correlation cutoff, and streak threshold in this codebase is defined as a named export in /content/scoring-thresholds.ts.
1. Import SCORING_THRESHOLDS from /content/scoring-thresholds.ts at the top of
any file containing scoring, analytics, or phase advancement logic.
3. Never write a number like 14, 0.3, 0.6, 4, 20, 30, or 60 directly into logic
code. Every one of these has a named constant.
4. If a value is needed that does not exist in the constants file, add it to
The reason: these values will be refined post-launch as member outcome data
accumulates. A named constant changed in one file propagates everywhere automatically.
A magic number requires finding every instance by hand — this will cause errors.
There are no exceptions to this rule.
9.5 — Quick Reference: All Logic Decisions by Section
Phase 1 answer supersedes intake if NOT NULL
Single driver → Dual → Strong secondary → Secondary → Fallback
Both assigned TRUE — masseter release + suboccipital tennis ball
3 or more of 4 NS flags confirmed
resistance_phase_start NOT NULL AND >= 4 weeks since phase2_completed_at
Phase 5 (not Phase 4 — Phase 4 is permanently accessible separately)
Only increments if completedSessionId matches current active session
Yes — not filtered by time window
First half vs second half of all logs by date
14 days from phase2_completed_at AND resistance phase started
Built to help people. Designed to last.
THE SOMATIC TINNITUS PROJECT — V2 Logic & Scoring Specifications — Document 13
